fr:title.text: Denotational semantics of the addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Denotational semantics of the addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
The denotational semantics of the addition language is given below:
\llbracket \text {Val}\ n \rrbracket = n \llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket x \rrbracket + \llbracket y \rrbracket

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Context

fr:title.text: An addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: An addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: This language has only one operator—addition—and one data type—the integers. The purpose of such a simply language is to illustrate concepts in programming language theory without the complexity of more realistic languages to distract us.

fr:title.text: The addition language grammar

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The addition language grammar

fr:title.text: The addition language defined as a data type

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The addition language defined as a data type

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
As a Haskell type, we can define the language this way:
data Expr = Val Int | Add Expr Expr

fr:title.text: Denotational semantics of the addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Denotational semantics of the addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
The denotational semantics of the addition language is given below:
\llbracket \text {Val}\ n \rrbracket = n \llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket x \rrbracket + \llbracket y \rrbracket

fr:title.text: The compositionality property of the addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The compositionality property of the addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
The compositionality of the language may be defined as follows:
\frac {\llbracket x \rrbracket = \llbracket x' \rrbracket \ \llbracket y \rrbracket = \llbracket y' \rrbracket } {\llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x'\ y' \rrbracket }

fr:title.text: Small-step operational semantics of the addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Small-step operational semantics of the addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The small-step operational semantics of the addition language is given below.

The first rule is the reduction rule (or contraction rule). It specified how the basic operation (here, addition) can be performed. Expressions matching this rule are called reducible expressions or redexes:
\frac {}{\text {Add}\ (\text {Val}\ n)\ (\text {Val}\ m) \rightarrow \text {Val}\ (n + m)}

fr:title.text: Preservation of denotation under small-step semantics of the addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Preservation of denotation under small-step semantics of the addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
A transition according to the small-step semantics of the language does not change the denotation:
\frac {e \rightarrow e'}{\llbracket e \rrbracket = \llbracket e' \rrbracket }

This property can be proved using structural induction on the structure of the expression e:
Base case
e = \text {Val}\ n
The base case is trivially true; it does not match any rule in the small-step semantics, because it is already in a terminal state and so there is no possible transition.
Inductive case
e = \text {Add}\ x\ y
If the precondition e \rightarrow e' has the form
\text {Add}\ (\text {Val}\ n) (\text {Val}\ m) \rightarrow \text {Val}\ (n+m)
then the conclusion
\llbracket \text {Add}\ (\text {Val}\ n) (\text {Val}\ m) \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Val}\ (n+m) \rrbracket
is true, because both sides evaluate to n+m.
If the precondition e \rightarrow e' has the form
\text {Add}\ x\ y \rightarrow \text {Add}\ x'\ y
for some transition x \rightarrow x', then we must show that
\llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x'\ y \rrbracket
is true. \llbracket x \rrbracket = \llbracket x' \rrbracket is true by induction—we assume the preservation property holds as our inductive hypothesis—and \llbracket y \rrbracket = \llbracket y' \rrbracket is true by reflexivity. Under compositionality, we conclude that \llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x'\ y \rrbracket .
If the precondition e \rightarrow e' has the form
\text {Add}\ x\ y \rightarrow \text {Add}\ x\ y'
for some transition y \rightarrow y', then we must show that
\llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y' \rrbracket
is true. The proof is the same as above. \llbracket x \rrbracket = \llbracket x \rrbracket is true by reflexivity and \llbracket y \rrbracket = \llbracket y' \rrbracket is true by induction. Therefore, \llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y' \rrbracket .

fr:title.text: Evaluation of expressions of the addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Evaluation of expressions of the addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
Evaluation under the small-step semantics for the addition language consists of zero or more transition steps. Formally, this is captured by taking the reflexitive transitive closure of the transition relation, written {\overset {*}{\rightarrow }}. The evaluation of e to e' may be writen
e \overset {*}{ \rightarrow } e' = e \rightarrow e_1 \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow e_n \rightarrow e'

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Backlinks

fr:title.text: Evaluation and reduction

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Evaluation and reduction

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Evaluation is a function or a process of computing semantic values from syntactic terms. It is denotational in nature. Evaluation should be distinguished from reduction, which is the process of computing a syntactic term from another syntactic term—usually a redex—that is equivalent, but having had some elimination rule applied. When a term can be reduced no further, it is a normal form.

fr:title.text: Structural induction

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Structural induction

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Structural induction is a syntax-directed proof style that defines a base case and an inductive case. The base case provides a term for which a property P holds, while the inductive case shows how—assuming P holds for some arbitrary subterm(s)—it also holds for a composition that includes these subterms.

Consider the case where you have an small addition language. Suppose we wish to prove that the denotational semantics of the language is total, that is, that that for every expression e there is an integer n such that \llbracket e \rrbracket = n. If we assume compositionality, we can prove totality holds using structural induction.

fr:title.text: The compositionality property of the addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The compositionality property of the addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
The compositionality of the language may be defined as follows:
\frac {\llbracket x \rrbracket = \llbracket x' \rrbracket \ \llbracket y \rrbracket = \llbracket y' \rrbracket } {\llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x'\ y' \rrbracket }
The base case is the constructor e = \text {Val}\ n.
This case is trivial given the semantics, specifically \llbracket \text {Val}\ n \rrbracket = n.
The inductive case is the constructor e = \text {Add}\ x\ y.

Assume \llbracket x \rrbracket = n and \llbracket y \rrbracket = m for some integers n and m. It follows from the valuation function that
\llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket x \rrbracket + \llbracket y \rrbracket = n + m
By assuming that the subterms of \text {Add} already satisfy totality—the so-called inductive hypothesis—we can prove that totality also holds for \text {Add}\ x\ y.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Related

fr:title.text: An addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: An addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: This language has only one operator—addition—and one data type—the integers. The purpose of such a simply language is to illustrate concepts in programming language theory without the complexity of more realistic languages to distract us.

fr:title.text: The addition language grammar

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The addition language grammar

fr:title.text: The addition language defined as a data type

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The addition language defined as a data type

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
As a Haskell type, we can define the language this way:
data Expr = Val Int | Add Expr Expr

fr:title.text: Denotational semantics of the addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Denotational semantics of the addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
The denotational semantics of the addition language is given below:
\llbracket \text {Val}\ n \rrbracket = n \llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket x \rrbracket + \llbracket y \rrbracket

fr:title.text: The compositionality property of the addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The compositionality property of the addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
The compositionality of the language may be defined as follows:
\frac {\llbracket x \rrbracket = \llbracket x' \rrbracket \ \llbracket y \rrbracket = \llbracket y' \rrbracket } {\llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x'\ y' \rrbracket }

fr:title.text: Small-step operational semantics of the addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Small-step operational semantics of the addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The small-step operational semantics of the addition language is given below.

The first rule is the reduction rule (or contraction rule). It specified how the basic operation (here, addition) can be performed. Expressions matching this rule are called reducible expressions or redexes:
\frac {}{\text {Add}\ (\text {Val}\ n)\ (\text {Val}\ m) \rightarrow \text {Val}\ (n + m)}

fr:title.text: Preservation of denotation under small-step semantics of the addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Preservation of denotation under small-step semantics of the addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
A transition according to the small-step semantics of the language does not change the denotation:
\frac {e \rightarrow e'}{\llbracket e \rrbracket = \llbracket e' \rrbracket }

This property can be proved using structural induction on the structure of the expression e:
Base case
e = \text {Val}\ n
The base case is trivially true; it does not match any rule in the small-step semantics, because it is already in a terminal state and so there is no possible transition.
Inductive case
e = \text {Add}\ x\ y
If the precondition e \rightarrow e' has the form
\text {Add}\ (\text {Val}\ n) (\text {Val}\ m) \rightarrow \text {Val}\ (n+m)
then the conclusion
\llbracket \text {Add}\ (\text {Val}\ n) (\text {Val}\ m) \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Val}\ (n+m) \rrbracket
is true, because both sides evaluate to n+m.
If the precondition e \rightarrow e' has the form
\text {Add}\ x\ y \rightarrow \text {Add}\ x'\ y
for some transition x \rightarrow x', then we must show that
\llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x'\ y \rrbracket
is true. \llbracket x \rrbracket = \llbracket x' \rrbracket is true by induction—we assume the preservation property holds as our inductive hypothesis—and \llbracket y \rrbracket = \llbracket y' \rrbracket is true by reflexivity. Under compositionality, we conclude that \llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x'\ y \rrbracket .
If the precondition e \rightarrow e' has the form
\text {Add}\ x\ y \rightarrow \text {Add}\ x\ y'
for some transition y \rightarrow y', then we must show that
\llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y' \rrbracket
is true. The proof is the same as above. \llbracket x \rrbracket = \llbracket x \rrbracket is true by reflexivity and \llbracket y \rrbracket = \llbracket y' \rrbracket is true by induction. Therefore, \llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y' \rrbracket .

fr:title.text: Evaluation of expressions of the addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Evaluation of expressions of the addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
Evaluation under the small-step semantics for the addition language consists of zero or more transition steps. Formally, this is captured by taking the reflexitive transitive closure of the transition relation, written {\overset {*}{\rightarrow }}. The evaluation of e to e' may be writen
e \overset {*}{ \rightarrow } e' = e \rightarrow e_1 \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow e_n \rightarrow e'

fr:title.text: Denotational semantics

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Denotational semantics

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
The denotational semantics defines the meanings of terms in a language T using a valuation function that maps syntactic terms in the syntactic domain T to semantic values in the semantic domain V. Usually, we enclose expressions in semantic brackets to express their denotation, e.g., for term e, we can write
\llbracket e \rrbracket
such that \llbracket - \rrbracket : T \to V.

The valuation function is compositional; the meaning of a compound term is defined purely in terms of the meanings of its subterms. This compositionality simplifies reasoning, allowing us to replace "equals by equals", i.e., we can substitute terms with other terms having the same meaning.
\frac {\llbracket x \rrbracket = \llbracket x' \rrbracket }{\llbracket f(x) \rrbracket = \llbracket f(x') \rrbracket }

Because terms and their semantics are built up inductively, proofs about denotation semantics typically proceed using structural induction. For instance, we can prove that our expression semantics is total: for every expression e in the language, there is a value v such that \llbracket e \rrbracket = v. The proof of totality proceeds by induction on the structure of the expression e.
